# Yelp Feedback Clustering Pipeline

## Project Overview
Production-oriented NLP pipeline for clustering and labelling Yelp reviews. Uses SentenceTransformer embeddings + BERTopic topic modelling + optional local LLM labelling via Ollama. Designed to be extensible (database, caching, async layers planned later).

## Stack
- **Embeddings**: `sentence-transformers` — `all-MiniLM-L6-v2`, L2-normalised float32, `(n_docs, 384)`
- **Topic modelling**: `BERTopic` with explicit UMAP + HDBSCAN construction
- **LLM labelling**: Ollama only (no Anthropic — keep costs zero). Model: `llama3.2:3b`
- **Config**: Pydantic `BaseSettings`, env prefix `YELP_`, `.env` file supported
- **Serialisation**: safetensors for BERTopic model persistence

## Environment
- Always use `.venv` — system Python is blocked by PEP 668 (Homebrew managed)
- Run with: `PYTHONPATH=.. .venv/bin/python -m yelp_feedback_pipeline.pipeline`
- Ollama runs as a brew service (`brew services start ollama`), model `llama3.2:3b` is pulled

## Project Structure
```
yelp_feedback_pipeline/
  config.py           # PipelineConfig (BaseSettings)
  models.py           # Review, TopicLabel, PipelineResult dataclasses
  pipeline.py         # FeedbackClusteringPipeline + CLI main()
  ingestion/
    loader.py         # Streams NDJSON line-by-line, returns typed DataFrame
  embedding/
    embedder.py       # ReviewEmbedder — lazy-loaded SentenceTransformer
  clustering/
    topic_model.py    # TopicModeller — UMAP + HDBSCAN + BERTopic
  labelling/
    labeller.py       # build_topic_labels(), custom_labeller hook
    llm_labeller.py   # OllamaLabeller, AnthropicLabeller, make_llm_labeller()
  data/               # Put yelp_academic_dataset_review.json here
  output/             # Pipeline outputs: CSVs, embeddings.npy, bertopic_model/
```

## Key Design Decisions
- Pre-computed embeddings passed into BERTopic separately so embedder and topic model are independently cacheable
- `custom_labeller: (keywords, scores) -> str` hook injected into `build_topic_labels()` — LLM is a drop-in
- `LLMLabeller.__call__()` catches all exceptions and falls back to keyword joining — pipeline never fails due to LLM errors
- topic_id == -1 is BERTopic outlier cluster, mapped to label "outlier"
- `nr_topics="auto"` used for automatic topic reduction

## Running the Pipeline
```bash
cd /Users/drewmcgee/workplace/yelp_feedback_pipeline

# Without LLM (keyword labels only)
PYTHONPATH=.. .venv/bin/python -m yelp_feedback_pipeline.pipeline \
  data/yelp_academic_dataset_review.json \
  --max-reviews 50000 \
  --min-topic-size 50 \
  --output-dir output/

# With Ollama LLM labelling (preferred)
PYTHONPATH=.. .venv/bin/python -m yelp_feedback_pipeline.pipeline \
  data/yelp_academic_dataset_review.json \
  --max-reviews 50000 \
  --min-topic-size 50 \
  --llm-labeller ollama \
  --llm-labeller-model llama3.2:3b \
  --output-dir output/
```

## Real Data
- Download from yelp.com/dataset (requires ToS acceptance, ~4GB tar)
- Extract `yelp_academic_dataset_review.json` → place in `data/`
- ~7 million reviews total; start with `--max-reviews 50000` for good topic quality

## Output Files
- `output/labelled_reviews.csv` — reviews with topic_id and topic_label columns
- `output/topic_summary.csv` — one row per topic with label and keyword list
- `output/embeddings.npy` — float32 embedding matrix
- `output/bertopic_model/` — serialised BERTopic model (safetensors)

## Cost Policy
No external API calls. Ollama local only. Do not add Anthropic or any other paid API to the default execution path.

# Yelp Feedback Clustering Pipeline

## Project Overview
Production-oriented NLP pipeline for clustering and labelling Yelp reviews. Uses SentenceTransformer embeddings + BERTopic topic modelling + optional local LLM labelling via Ollama. Designed to be extensible (database, caching, async layers planned later).

## Stack
- **Embeddings**: `sentence-transformers` — `all-MiniLM-L6-v2`, L2-normalised float32, `(n_docs, 384)`
- **Topic modelling**: `BERTopic` with explicit UMAP + HDBSCAN construction
- **LLM labelling**: Ollama only (no Anthropic — keep costs zero). Model: `llama3.2:3b`
- **Config**: Pydantic `BaseSettings`, env prefix `YELP_`, `.env` file supported
- **Serialisation**: safetensors for BERTopic model persistence

## Environment
- Always use `.venv` — system Python is blocked by PEP 668 (Homebrew managed)
- Run with: `PYTHONPATH=.. .venv/bin/python -m yelp_feedback_pipeline.pipeline`
- Ollama runs as a brew service (`brew services start ollama`), model `llama3.2:3b` is pulled

## Project Structure
```
yelp_feedback_pipeline/
  config.py           # PipelineConfig (BaseSettings)
  models.py           # Review, TopicLabel, PipelineResult dataclasses
  pipeline.py         # FeedbackClusteringPipeline + CLI main()
  ingestion/
    loader.py         # Streams NDJSON line-by-line, returns typed DataFrame
  embedding/
    embedder.py       # ReviewEmbedder — lazy-loaded SentenceTransformer
  clustering/
    topic_model.py    # TopicModeller — UMAP + HDBSCAN + BERTopic
  labelling/
    labeller.py       # build_topic_labels(), custom_labeller hook
    llm_labeller.py   # OllamaLabeller, AnthropicLabeller, make_llm_labeller()
  data/               # Put yelp_academic_dataset_review.json here
  output/             # Pipeline outputs: CSVs, embeddings.npy, bertopic_model/
```

## Key Design Decisions
- Pre-computed embeddings passed into BERTopic separately so embedder and topic model are independently cacheable
- `custom_labeller: (keywords, scores) -> str` hook injected into `build_topic_labels()` — LLM is a drop-in
- `LLMLabeller.__call__()` catches all exceptions and falls back to keyword joining — pipeline never fails due to LLM errors
- topic_id == -1 is BERTopic outlier cluster, mapped to label "outlier"
- `nr_topics="auto"` used for automatic topic reduction

## Running the Pipeline
```bash
cd /Users/drewmcgee/workplace/yelp_feedback_pipeline

# Without LLM (keyword labels only)
PYTHONPATH=.. .venv/bin/python -m yelp_feedback_pipeline.pipeline \
  data/yelp_academic_dataset_review.json \
  --max-reviews 50000 \
  --min-topic-size 50 \
  --output-dir output/

# With Ollama LLM labelling (preferred)
PYTHONPATH=.. .venv/bin/python -m yelp_feedback_pipeline.pipeline \
  data/yelp_academic_dataset_review.json \
  --max-reviews 50000 \
  --min-topic-size 50 \
  --llm-labeller ollama \
  --llm-labeller-model llama3.2:3b \
  --output-dir output/
```

## Real Data
- Download from yelp.com/dataset (requires ToS acceptance, ~4GB tar)
- Extract `yelp_academic_dataset_review.json` → place in `data/`
- ~7 million reviews total; start with `--max-reviews 50000` for good topic quality

## Output Files
- `output/labelled_reviews.csv` — reviews with topic_id and topic_label columns
- `output/topic_summary.csv` — one row per topic with label and keyword list
- `output/embeddings.npy` — float32 embedding matrix
- `output/bertopic_model/` — serialised BERTopic model (safetensors)

## Cost Policy
No external API calls. Ollama local only. Do not add Anthropic or any other paid API to the default execution path.

content.text:
No — the Yelp dataset requires manually accepting their Terms of Service on the Yelp website before a download link is issued. There's no way to automate that step (no public direct URL, ToS wall).

**What you need to do:**

1. Go to `yelp.com/dataset`
2. Click "Download Dataset", fill in your name/email, accept the ToS
3. You'll get a download link for a `~4GB` tar file
4. Extract it and place `yelp_academic_dataset_review.json` in `data/`

Once the file is in `data/`, you're ready to run the pipeline as documented in [CLAUDE.md](CLAUDE.md).
is that THE dataset to use for this or are there other great options?

For Yelp review clustering/NLP specifically, the Yelp dataset is the gold standard — it's real, large-scale, and domain-matched to exactly what the pipeline is built for.

Other strong options depending on what you're trying to do:

**Also restaurant/business reviews:**
- **Google Places reviews** — scrapable via the Places API but rate-limited and requires a key
- **TripAdvisor** — no official public dataset; scraping violates ToS

**General review datasets (good for testing the pipeline generically):**
- **Amazon Reviews (McAuley datasets)** — publicly available, massive, well-structured NDJSON, very similar format to the Yelp dataset. Probably the easiest drop-in alternative
- **IMDB / MovieLens reviews** — smaller, different domain

**If you just want to test the pipeline without real data:**
- `generate_test_data.py` is already in the repo — generates synthetic NDJSON reviews locally, no download needed

**My recommendation:** use `generate_test_data.py` to validate the pipeline end-to-end now, then get the real Yelp dataset when you want production-quality topic clusters. The Amazon reviews dataset is the best fallback if you want real data without the Yelp ToS friction.

content.thinking:
The user is asking about structured outputs with Claude/Anthropic API. This is a question about the Claude API capabilities. Let me explain what's available.

Claude supports structured outputs in a few ways:
1. **Tool use / function calling** — you define a JSON schema via tools, and Claude is forced to output valid JSON matching that schema. This is the most reliable way.
2. **Prefill** — you can prefill the assistant turn with `{` to nudge Claude toward JSON
3. **Prompt instructions** — ask Claude to output JSON, but this is less reliable

For the specific use case of "nailing statistics without hallucination" — structured output helps with *format* but doesn't prevent hallucination of *values*. 

I'm realizing the user might be mixing up two separate concerns here: structured output ensures valid JSON with the right fields, but it won't stop Claude from inventing statistics if they're not actually known. The real solution is to ground the data in the prompt itself, then use structured output just to parse and reformat what's already there.

content.text:
Yes — via **tool use** (function calling), which is the most reliable method. You define a JSON schema and Claude is constrained to return output matching it exactly.

But there's an important distinction:

**Structured output solves format, not hallucination.** If you want Claude to "nail statistics you put in the prompt", structured output alone isn't the fix — the real pattern is:

1. **Put the stats in the prompt** (grounding — Claude extracts, not recalls)
2. **Use a schema to force structured extraction** (so the output is parseable and complete)

This way Claude is doing *parsing*, not *remembering* — hallucination risk drops dramatically.

**Example with the Anthropic SDK:**
```python
import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

tools = [{
    "name": "record_stats",
    "description": "Record extracted statistics",
    "input_schema": {
        "type": "object",
        "properties": {
            "average_rating": {"type": "number"},
            "review_count": {"type": "integer"},
            "top_complaint": {"type": "string"}
        },
        "required": ["average_rating", "review_count", "top_complaint"]
    }
}]

response = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
    max_tokens=1024,
    tools=tools,
    tool_choice={"type": "tool", "name": "record_stats"},  # forces it
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": f"Extract stats from: {your_data}"}]
)

result = response.content[0].input  # guaranteed valid dict
```

`tool_choice: {"type": "tool", "name": "..."}` forces Claude to use that schema — no prose, no deviation.

**For your pipeline specifically** — if you wanted Claude to extract topic statistics from reviews and return structured results, this is exactly how you'd do it. Want me to wire this into the pipeline somewhere?
so that nails the formats and types but how do we nail the exact values? i though we could structure output on specific values

content.thinking:
The user is asking about how to ensure Claude returns exact/correct values, not just the right format. They thought structured outputs could constrain the values too.

Let me think about what's actually available:

1. **JSON Schema enum** — you can constrain string fields to specific allowed values using `enum`. This is a real feature of JSON Schema and works with Claude's tool use.
2. **const** — JSON Schema also supports `const` for a single allowed value
3. **minimum/maximum** for numbers
4. **minLength/maxLength** for strings
5. **pattern** for regex constraints on strings

So yes, you CAN constrain values using JSON Schema features within tool definitions. For example:


- `"enum": ["positive", "negative", "neutral"]` forces one of those three values
- `"minimum": 1, "maximum": 5` forces a rating between 1-5
- `"pattern": "^[A-Z]{2}$"` forces a 2-letter uppercase string

But the real issue is ensuring Claude doesn't hallucinate specific values — like if you give it statistics and want it to return exactly those numbers. For that, `enum` works if the values are known ahead of time, but for dynamic values extracted from text, you're relying on grounding by providing the data directly in the prompt.

Now I'm thinking about how these schema constraints actually apply within tool definitions themselves.

content.text:
You can constrain values using JSON Schema features inside your tool definition. Claude will respect them:

**`enum`** — the most powerful for exact values:
```python
"sentiment": {
    "type": "string",
    "enum": ["positive", "negative", "neutral"]  # only these 3 are valid
}
```

**`minimum`/`maximum`** for numbers:
```python
"rating": {
    "type": "number",
    "minimum": 1,
    "maximum": 5
}
```

**`pattern`** for regex-constrained strings:
```python
"date": {
    "type": "string",
    "pattern": "^\\d{4}-\\d{2}-\\d{2}$"  # YYYY-MM-DD only
}
```

**But here's the ceiling:** these constraints work for *categories and ranges*, not for *free-form values you want extracted exactly right*. If you have a stat like "average rating: 3.87" in the prompt and want Claude to return exactly `3.87` — JSON Schema can't enforce that. The only reliable mechanism there is **grounding** (the data is in the prompt, Claude is parsing not recalling).

**The combination that actually nails it:**

| Goal | Mechanism |
|---|---|
| Right format/type | Tool schema |
| Value from a known set | `enum` |
| Value in a range | `min`/`max` |
| Value from source data | Grounding in prompt |
| Free-form extraction accuracy | Grounding + validation in your code |

So for your stats use case: put the numbers in the prompt, use `enum` where values are categorical, add range constraints where applicable, then validate extracted numbers against the source in Python before trusting them.
weird why cant you variably defined a number or string for the output where its like the average_rating: {rating[i]} wouldnt that be useful

Yeah it's a reasonable intuition but it breaks the whole point of the schema — the schema is a **contract defined before the call**, so Claude (and your deserialiser) both know the shape upfront. You can't have a schema that says "the value must equal X" where X is only known at runtime... because then why is Claude computing it at all?

The two cases where this might feel useful:

**Case 1: You want to verify Claude extracted a value correctly**
Don't do it in the schema — do it in your code after:
```python
result = response.content[0].input
assert result["average_rating"] == expected_rating, "Claude hallucinated"
```

**Case 2: You want Claude to pick from a dynamic set of values**
Build the `enum` at runtime before the call:
```python
tools[0]["input_schema"]["properties"]["category"]["enum"] = list(known_categories)
```
That's actually a legit pattern — dynamic enum built from your data, injected into the schema before sending.

**The real insight:** if you already know what value Claude *should* return, you don't need Claude to return it — you just use the value directly. Structured output is for when Claude is *computing or extracting* something, and you want the result in a reliable shape. The accuracy of the value itself comes from grounding, not the schema.
no that is the point. claude is NOT computing the statistic. we are talking about the usecase where statistics are fed to claude in order to include in a nice response. like a chatbot usecase

Ah got it — yeah that's a real problem. You feed Claude "average rating is 3.87" and it writes a response that says "3.92" because it paraphrased carelessly.

Structured output doesn't solve this because the schema can't say "this field must equal the value I mentioned in the prompt."

**What actually works for this use case:**

**1. Don't let Claude touch the numbers at all — template them in yourself**
```python
stats = {"average_rating": 3.87, "review_count": 1204}

prose = client.messages.create(...,
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": 
        "Write a summary. Do not include any numbers, I will add them."}]
)

final = prose.text.replace("{{average_rating}}", str(stats["average_rating"]))
```

**2. Extract the prose and numbers separately, then merge**
Claude writes the narrative with placeholders, you inject the real values:
```python
# Prompt: "Write a summary using {{average_rating}} and {{review_count}} as placeholders"
# Claude returns: "Customers love this place with an average of {{average_rating}} stars..."
# You substitute the real values in Python
```

**3. Post-call validation + retry**
Use structured output to extract what Claude *claims* the stats are, diff against source, retry if wrong.

**The cleanest real-world pattern is option 2** — Claude handles language, your code handles numbers. Never trust Claude to faithfully transcribe a specific numeric value when it can just be templated. The LLM is doing the wrong job when you ask it to copy numbers accurately.
ok and do you use structured output to guaranteee is include {{average_rating}}? couldnt that be hallucinatied away too? or its representation is much more robust than a numbers

Exactly right — and yes, structured output is the fix here.

You'd have Claude return the prose as a structured field, which forces it to produce the full string including the placeholder:

```python
tools = [{
    "name": "write_summary",
    "input_schema": {
        "type": "object",
        "properties": {
            "summary": {"type": "string"}
        },
        "required": ["summary"]
    }
}]
# Prompt: "Write a summary using {{average_rating}} and {{review_count}} as placeholders"
# Claude returns: {"summary": "Customers love this place with {{average_rating}} stars..."}
```

Then validate before substituting:

```python
summary = response.content[0].input["summary"]
assert "{{average_rating}}" in summary  # catch if Claude dropped it
assert "{{review_count}}" in summary
final = summary.replace("{{average_rating}}", str(stats["average_rating"]))
```

**Why placeholders are more robust than numbers:**

- Numbers are semantically loaded — Claude "knows" ratings are ~1-5 and may autocorrect
- `{{average_rating}}` is just an opaque token to Claude, no semantic pull to change it
- But it can still be dropped or mangled if Claude decides to rephrase

So the full robust pattern is: **structured output to get the string back in a parseable field + assertion that all placeholders survived + your code does the substitution.** Claude handles language, Python handles data. Clean separation.

Honest answer: **not much**. The codebase is fairly thin on gymnastics — here's what's actually there and what's interesting vs boring:

**Worth looking at:**

- [embedding/embedder.py:71-79](embedding/embedder.py#L71-L79) — L2 normalisation with `np.linalg.norm` broadcasting, and explicit `.astype(np.float32)` cast. Small but a real pattern.

- [ingestion/loader.py:112-113](ingestion/loader.py#L112-L113) — `pd.DataFrame(records)` from a list of dicts + `pd.to_datetime()` parsing.

**Boring/thin:**
- `np.save()` in pipeline.py — just serialisation, nothing interesting
- Most of the pandas usage is just passing DataFrames between pipeline stages

- Filtering outlier clusters (`df[df.topic_id != -1]`)
- Computing per-topic stats with `groupby`
- Cosine similarity between topic centroids using matrix ops

content.text:
Topics are sensible given the keywords — hair salons, donuts, nail salons, car services, dentists, gyms, bakeries, tours. Topic 0 has junk keywords (`the, and, to`) which is a stopword filtering issue worth fixing.

The **46% outlier rate** and **noisy topic 0** are the two things to improve — both are solvable by running on more reviews (50K) and tweaking the UMAP/HDBSCAN params. Want to run with Ollama labelling on to get better human-readable labels for these topics?

weird i though this was a semantic embedding based approach where stopwords werent relevant. 

help me get to the bottom of this... are we NOT using sentence embeddings?

content.thinking:
The user raises a good point. BERTopic uses sentence embeddings for the clustering/UMAP/HDBSCAN part, but the topic *representation* (the keywords you see in the output) is done via c-TF-IDF, which is a bag-of-words approach applied to the documents in each cluster. So the clustering IS semantic, but the keyword labels are not — they're TF-IDF based and will include stopwords.

Let me read the topic_model.py and labeller.py to confirm this and explain it properly.

    18	        "was", "for", "on", "at", "with", "this", "that", "i", "my",
    19	        "we", "they", "their", "our", "be", "are", "have", "has", "had",

    21	        "get", "got", "go", "went",
    22	    }
    23	)
    24	
    25	
    26	def _clean_keyword(word: str) -> str:
    27	    """Strip punctuation and lowercase."""
    28	    return re.sub(r"[^a-z0-9\-]", "", word.lower())
    29	
    30	
    31	def _keywords_to_label(
    32	    keywords: list[str],
    33	    scores: list[float],
    34	    max_words: int = 3,
    35	    separator: str = " | ",
    36	    stopwords: frozenset[str] = _LABEL_STOPWORDS,
    37	) -> str:
    38	    """
    39	    Produce a human-readable label from the top-N c-TF-IDF keywords.
    40	
    41	    Strategy: drop stopwords, keep the highest-scoring unique terms,
    42	    join with ``separator``.
    43	    """
    44	    seen: set[str] = set()
    45	    chosen: list[str] = []
    46	
    47	    for word, score in sorted(zip(keywords, scores), key=lambda x: -x[1]):
    48	        clean = _clean_keyword(word)
    49	        if not clean or clean in stopwords or clean in seen:
    50	            continue
    51	        seen.add(clean)
    52	        chosen.append(clean)
    53	        if len(chosen) >= max_words:
    54	            break
    55	
    56	    return separator.join(chosen) if chosen else "topic"
    57	
    58	
    59	def build_topic_labels(
    60	    topic_modeller: TopicModeller,
    61	    *,
    62	    top_n_words: int = 10,

    65	) -> list[TopicLabel]:
    66	    """
    67	    Extract :class:`TopicLabel` objects from a fitted :class:`TopicModeller`.

    69	    Parameters

    71	    topic_modeller:

    73	    top_n_words:

    75	    label_separator:
    76	        Separator used when joining keywords into a label string.
    77	    custom_labeller:

    79	        default keyword-joining strategy (e.g. call an LLM).
    80	
    81	    Returns

    83	    List of :class:`TopicLabel`, sorted by topic_id.
    84	    """
    85	    topic_info = topic_modeller.get_topic_info()
    86	    labels: list[TopicLabel] = []
    87	
    88	    for row in topic_info:

    91

    99	            keywords, scores = [], []

   120	        )

content.text:
Your instinct is right but the architecture is actually correct — there are **two separate layers** doing different jobs:

**Clustering: fully semantic (embeddings)**
- `fit_transform()` at [topic_model.py:116](clustering/topic_model.py#L116) passes your pre-computed sentence embeddings directly into BERTopic
- UMAP reduces the 384-dim embedding space, HDBSCAN clusters in that space
- Stopwords are irrelevant here — "the" and "restaurant" are equidistant in embedding space

**Keywords: bag-of-words (c-TF-IDF)**
- After clustering semantically, BERTopic *describes* each cluster using c-TF-IDF over the raw text of documents in that cluster
- c-TF-IDF is just term frequency — it has no idea about semantics, so stopwords dominate
- This is where `the, and, to` come from

So the stopword problem is only in the **keyword labels**, not the clusters themselves. The `_LABEL_STOPWORDS` filter in [labeller.py:15-23](labelling/labeller.py#L15-L23) is already stripping them from the *label string* — but the raw `keywords` column in the CSV still shows them because that's the unfiltered c-TF-IDF output.

**The real fix** is telling BERTopic's vectoriser to exclude stopwords at source, so they never appear in keywords at all:

```python
from sklearn.feature_extraction.text import CountVectorizer

vectorizer = CountVectorizer(stop_words="english")
self.model = BERTopic(..., vectorizer_model=vectorizer)
```

Want me to add that to `topic_model.py`?

from __future__ import annotations

import logging
from pathlib import Path
from typing import Optional, Union

import numpy as np
from bertopic import BERTopic
from hdbscan import HDBSCAN
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer
from umap import UMAP

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)


def _build_umap(
    n_components: int,
    n_neighbors: int,
    metric: str,
    random_state: int = 42,
) -> UMAP:
    return UMAP(
        n_components=n_components,
        n_neighbors=n_neighbors,
        metric=metric,
        low_memory=False,
        random_state=random_state,
    )


def _build_hdbscan(min_cluster_size: int) -> HDBSCAN:
    return HDBSCAN(
        min_cluster_size=min_cluster_size,
        metric="euclidean",          # UMAP output is already low-dim euclidean space
        cluster_selection_method="eom",
        prediction_data=True,        # required for soft-cluster probabilities
    )


class TopicModeller:
    """
    Production wrapper around BERTopic.

    Design choices
    --------------
    - Pre-computed embeddings are passed in so that the embedder can be
      swapped or cached independently of the topic model.
    - UMAP and HDBSCAN are constructed explicitly so hyperparams are
      visible and testable without digging into BERTopic internals.
    - The raw BERTopic model is exposed via ``self.model`` for callers
      that need low-level access (e.g. ``visualize_topics``).
    """

    def __init__(
        self,
        embedding_model: SentenceTransformer,
        nr_topics: Union[int, str, None] = "auto",
        min_topic_size: int = 30,
        umap_n_components: int = 5,
        umap_n_neighbors: int = 15,
        umap_metric: str = "cosine",
        top_n_words: int = 10,
    ) -> None:
        self.nr_topics = nr_topics
        self.min_topic_size = min_topic_size
        self.top_n_words = top_n_words

        umap_model = _build_umap(umap_n_components, umap_n_neighbors, umap_metric)
        hdbscan_model = _build_hdbscan(min_topic_size)

        self.model = BERTopic(
            embedding_model=embedding_model,
            umap_model=umap_model,
            hdbscan_model=hdbscan_model,
            nr_topics=nr_topics,
            top_n_words=top_n_words,
            calculate_probabilities=True,
            verbose=True,
        )
        self._fitted = False

    # ------------------------------------------------------------------
    # Fit / transform
    # ------------------------------------------------------------------

    def fit_transform(
        self,
        texts: list[str],
        embeddings: np.ndarray,
    ) -> tuple[np.ndarray, np.ndarray]:
        """
        Fit the topic model and return per-document assignments.

        Parameters
        ----------
        texts:
            Raw review strings (same order as ``embeddings``).
        embeddings:
            Pre-computed embeddings from :class:`ReviewEmbedder`,
            shape ``(n_docs, embedding_dim)``.

        Returns
        -------
        topics : np.ndarray, shape (n_docs,)
            Topic ID per document; -1 = outlier.
        probs : np.ndarray, shape (n_docs,) or (n_docs, n_topics)
            Probability of topic assignment.
        """
        logger.info(
            "Fitting BERTopic on %d documents (nr_topics=%s, min_topic_size=%d)",
            len(texts),
            self.nr_topics,
            self.min_topic_size,
        )

        topics, probs = self.model.fit_transform(texts, embeddings=embeddings)
        self._fitted = True

        topics_arr = np.asarray(topics, dtype=np.int32)
        probs_arr = np.asarray(probs, dtype=np.float32)

        n_topics = len(set(topics_arr)) - (1 if -1 in topics_arr else 0)
        n_outliers = int((topics_arr == -1).sum())
        logger.info(
            "BERTopic complete — %d topics found, %d outlier docs (%.1f%%)",
            n_topics,
            n_outliers,
            100 * n_outliers / len(topics_arr),
        )

        return topics_arr, probs_arr

    def get_topic_info(self) -> list[dict]:
        """Return BERTopic's topic_info as a list of dicts."""
        self._require_fitted()
        return self.model.get_topic_info().to_dict("records")

    def get_topic_words(self, topic_id: int) -> list[tuple[str, float]]:
        """Return [(word, score), ...] for a given topic."""
        self._require_fitted()
        return self.model.get_topic(topic_id) or []

    # ------------------------------------------------------------------
    # Persistence
    # ------------------------------------------------------------------

    def save(self, directory: Path) -> Path:
        """Persist the fitted model to ``directory/bertopic_model``."""
        self._require_fitted()
        directory.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
        save_path = directory / "bertopic_model"
        self.model.save(str(save_path), serialization="safetensors", save_ctfidf=True)
        logger.info("BERTopic model saved to %s", save_path)
        return save_path

    @classmethod
    def load(cls, path: Path, embedding_model: SentenceTransformer) -> "TopicModeller":
        """Load a previously saved model from ``path``."""
        instance = cls.__new__(cls)
        instance.model = BERTopic.load(str(path), embedding_model=embedding_model)
        instance._fitted = True
        logger.info("BERTopic model loaded from %s", path)
        return instance

    # ------------------------------------------------------------------
    # Internal helpers
    # ------------------------------------------------------------------

    def _require_fitted(self) -> None:
        if not self._fitted:
            raise RuntimeError("TopicModeller has not been fitted yet. Call fit_transform first.")

from __future__ import annotations

import logging
from pathlib import Path
from typing import Optional, Union

import numpy as np
from bertopic import BERTopic
from hdbscan import HDBSCAN
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer
from umap import UMAP

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)


def _build_umap(
    n_components: int,
    n_neighbors: int,
    metric: str,
    random_state: int = 42,
) -> UMAP:
    return UMAP(
        n_components=n_components,
        n_neighbors=n_neighbors,
        metric=metric,
        low_memory=False,
        random_state=random_state,
    )


def _build_hdbscan(min_cluster_size: int) -> HDBSCAN:
    return HDBSCAN(
        min_cluster_size=min_cluster_size,
        metric="euclidean",          # UMAP output is already low-dim euclidean space
        cluster_selection_method="eom",
        prediction_data=True,        # required for soft-cluster probabilities
    )


class TopicModeller:
    """
    Production wrapper around BERTopic.

    Design choices
    --------------
    - Pre-computed embeddings are passed in so that the embedder can be
      swapped or cached independently of the topic model.
    - UMAP and HDBSCAN are constructed explicitly so hyperparams are
      visible and testable without digging into BERTopic internals.
    - The raw BERTopic model is exposed via ``self.model`` for callers
      that need low-level access (e.g. ``visualize_topics``).
    """

    def __init__(
        self,
        embedding_model: SentenceTransformer,
        nr_topics: Union[int, str, None] = "auto",
        min_topic_size: int = 30,
        umap_n_components: int = 5,
        umap_n_neighbors: int = 15,
        umap_metric: str = "cosine",
        top_n_words: int = 10,
    ) -> None:
        self.nr_topics = nr_topics
        self.min_topic_size = min_topic_size
        self.top_n_words = top_n_words

        umap_model = _build_umap(umap_n_components, umap_n_neighbors, umap_metric)
        hdbscan_model = _build_hdbscan(min_topic_size)

        self.model = BERTopic(
            embedding_model=embedding_model,
            umap_model=umap_model,
            hdbscan_model=hdbscan_model,
            nr_topics=nr_topics,
            top_n_words=top_n_words,
            calculate_probabilities=True,
            verbose=True,
        )
        self._fitted = False

    # ------------------------------------------------------------------
    # Fit / transform
    # ------------------------------------------------------------------

    def fit_transform(
        self,
        texts: list[str],
        embeddings: np.ndarray,
    ) -> tuple[np.ndarray, np.ndarray]:
        """
        Fit the topic model and return per-document assignments.

        Parameters
        ----------
        texts:
            Raw review strings (same order as ``embeddings``).
        embeddings:
            Pre-computed embeddings from :class:`ReviewEmbedder`,
            shape ``(n_docs, embedding_dim)``.

        Returns
        -------
        topics : np.ndarray, shape (n_docs,)
            Topic ID per document; -1 = outlier.
        probs : np.ndarray, shape (n_docs,) or (n_docs, n_topics)
            Probability of topic assignment.
        """
        logger.info(
            "Fitting BERTopic on %d documents (nr_topics=%s, min_topic_size=%d)",
            len(texts),
            self.nr_topics,
            self.min_topic_size,
        )

        topics, probs = self.model.fit_transform(texts, embeddings=embeddings)
        self._fitted = True

        topics_arr = np.asarray(topics, dtype=np.int32)
        probs_arr = np.asarray(probs, dtype=np.float32)

        n_topics = len(set(topics_arr)) - (1 if -1 in topics_arr else 0)
        n_outliers = int((topics_arr == -1).sum())
        logger.info(
            "BERTopic complete — %d topics found, %d outlier docs (%.1f%%)",
            n_topics,
            n_outliers,
            100 * n_outliers / len(topics_arr),
        )

        return topics_arr, probs_arr

    def get_topic_info(self) -> list[dict]:
        """Return BERTopic's topic_info as a list of dicts."""
        self._require_fitted()
        return self.model.get_topic_info().to_dict("records")

    def get_topic_words(self, topic_id: int) -> list[tuple[str, float]]:
        """Return [(word, score), ...] for a given topic."""
        self._require_fitted()
        return self.model.get_topic(topic_id) or []

    # ------------------------------------------------------------------
    # Persistence
    # ------------------------------------------------------------------

    def save(self, directory: Path) -> Path:
        """Persist the fitted model to ``directory/bertopic_model``."""
        self._require_fitted()
        directory.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
        save_path = directory / "bertopic_model"
        self.model.save(str(save_path), serialization="safetensors", save_ctfidf=True)
        logger.info("BERTopic model saved to %s", save_path)
        return save_path

    @classmethod
    def load(cls, path: Path, embedding_model: SentenceTransformer) -> "TopicModeller":
        """Load a previously saved model from ``path``."""
        instance = cls.__new__(cls)
        instance.model = BERTopic.load(str(path), embedding_model=embedding_model)
        instance._fitted = True
        logger.info("BERTopic model loaded from %s", path)
        return instance

    # ------------------------------------------------------------------
    # Internal helpers
    # ------------------------------------------------------------------

    def _require_fitted(self) -> None:
        if not self._fitted:
            raise RuntimeError("TopicModeller has not been fitted yet. Call fit_transform first.")

<ide_opened_file>The user opened the file /temp/readonly/command (ewefm7) in the IDE. This may or may not be related to the current task.</ide_opened_file>
so explain what you did here? the count vectorizer has a canned abiity to filter english stopwords

Exactly. `CountVectorizer(stop_words="english")` is a sklearn built-in — it ships with a hardcoded list of 318 common English stopwords (`the`, `and`, `to`, `is`, etc.) and drops them before computing term frequencies.

The key thing to understand is **where** it plugs in. BERTopic's pipeline is:

```
embeddings → UMAP → HDBSCAN → clusters
                                  ↓
              raw text → CountVectorizer → c-TF-IDF → keywords per cluster
```

The vectoriser only touches the **keyword extraction step**, not the clustering. So by injecting one with `stop_words="english"`, stopwords are excluded from the vocabulary entirely before c-TF-IDF even runs — they can never appear as top keywords regardless of how frequent they are.

Previously BERTopic was using its default `CountVectorizer()` with no stopword list, so `the` and `and` would dominate every topic simply because they appear in almost every review — high frequency but zero discriminative value.

The one caveat: sklearn's `"english"` list is general-purpose. For a Yelp-specific corpus you'd eventually want to also filter domain-noise words like `place`, `time`, `just`, `really` that are still showing up in topic 0. That's done by passing a custom list instead:

```python
CountVectorizer(stop_words=["the", "and", ..., "place", "time", "really"])
```

But the canned `"english"` list gets you 90% of the way there for free.

from __future__ import annotations

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import datetime
from typing import Optional

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd


@dataclass(slots=True)
class Review:
    review_id: str
    business_id: str
    user_id: str
    stars: float
    text: str
    date: datetime
    useful: int = 0
    funny: int = 0
    cool: int = 0


@dataclass(slots=True)
class TopicLabel:
    topic_id: int
    keywords: list[str]
    keyword_scores: list[float]
    label: str  # human-readable e.g. "food | service | wait"
    doc_count: int


@dataclass
class PipelineResult:
    reviews: pd.DataFrame
    embeddings: np.ndarray
    topic_assignments: np.ndarray        # shape (n_docs,) — topic_id per doc
    topic_probabilities: np.ndarray      # shape (n_docs, n_topics) or (n_docs,)
    topic_labels: list[TopicLabel]
    outlier_count: int                   # docs assigned topic_id == -1
    run_metadata: dict = field(default_factory=dict)

    @property
    def labelled_reviews(self) -> pd.DataFrame:
        """Return reviews DataFrame with topic_id and topic_label columns attached."""
        df = self.reviews.copy()
        df["topic_id"] = self.topic_assignments
        id_to_label = {t.topic_id: t.label for t in self.topic_labels}
        df["topic_label"] = df["topic_id"].map(id_to_label).fillna("outlier")
        return df

Yes — [models.py:12](models.py#L12). It's a Python `dataclass` with `slots=True`.

Three models in total:

- **`Review`** — one parsed Yelp row. The loader maps the raw JSON dict fields onto this, with type coercion (`float(stars)`, `datetime.strptime(date)`)
- **`TopicLabel`** — one topic's metadata after clustering: id, keywords, scores, human label, doc count
- **`PipelineResult`** — the full output of a pipeline run, holding everything together. The `labelled_reviews` property at [models.py:43](models.py#L43) is where pandas gymnastics actually happens — it joins `topic_assignments` back onto the reviews DataFrame and maps topic ids to labels.

The `slots=True` on `Review` and `TopicLabel` is a small memory optimisation — with 5-7M reviews it avoids each instance having a `__dict__`, which adds up.

from __future__ import annotations

import json
import logging
from datetime import datetime
from pathlib import Path
from typing import Iterator, Optional

import pandas as pd

from yelp_feedback_pipeline.models import Review

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

_DATE_FMT = "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S"


def _parse_review(raw: dict) -> Optional[Review]:
    """Parse a raw Yelp review dict; return None on malformed rows."""
    try:
        return Review(
            review_id=raw["review_id"],
            business_id=raw["business_id"],
            user_id=raw["user_id"],
            stars=float(raw["stars"]),
            text=raw["text"],
            date=datetime.strptime(raw["date"], _DATE_FMT),
            useful=int(raw.get("useful", 0)),
            funny=int(raw.get("funny", 0)),
            cool=int(raw.get("cool", 0)),
        )
    except (KeyError, ValueError, TypeError) as exc:
        logger.debug("Skipping malformed review: %s", exc)
        return None


def _stream_reviews(path: Path) -> Iterator[dict]:
    """Yield raw dicts from the newline-delimited JSON file."""
    with path.open("r", encoding="utf-8") as fh:
        for line in fh:
            line = line.strip()
            if line:
                try:
                    yield json.loads(line)
                except json.JSONDecodeError as exc:
                    logger.warning("JSON decode error — skipping line: %s", exc)


def load_reviews(
    path: Path,
    *,
    max_reviews: Optional[int] = None,
    min_review_length: int = 20,
    business_ids: Optional[set[str]] = None,
) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """
    Load and filter Yelp reviews from the NDJSON dataset file.

    Parameters
    ----------
    path:
        Absolute path to ``yelp_academic_dataset_review.json``.
    max_reviews:
        Hard cap on the number of reviews returned (after filtering).
        ``None`` means unlimited.
    min_review_length:
        Minimum character length for ``text``; shorter reviews are dropped.
    business_ids:
        Optional allow-list of business IDs.  ``None`` keeps all businesses.

    Returns
    -------
    pd.DataFrame with one row per review and columns matching :class:`Review`.
    """
    records: list[dict] = []
    skipped = 0

    for raw in _stream_reviews(path):
        if business_ids and raw.get("business_id") not in business_ids:
            continue

        review = _parse_review(raw)
        if review is None:
            skipped += 1
            continue

        if len(review.text) < min_review_length:
            skipped += 1
            continue

        records.append(
            {
                "review_id": review.review_id,
                "business_id": review.business_id,
                "user_id": review.user_id,
                "stars": review.stars,
                "text": review.text,
                "date": review.date,
                "useful": review.useful,
                "funny": review.funny,
                "cool": review.cool,
            }
        )

        if max_reviews and len(records) >= max_reviews:
            break

    logger.info(
        "Loaded %d reviews (%d skipped) from %s", len(records), skipped, path.name
    )

    df = pd.DataFrame(records)
    df["date"] = pd.to_datetime(df["date"])
    df["stars"] = df["stars"].astype("float32")
    return df

can you explain this fix conceptually im an SDE who wants to understnad

Sure. The issue is that `yelp_feedback_pipeline` is a package living at:

```
/Users/drewmcgee/workplace/yelp_feedback_pipeline/
```

For Python to resolve `from yelp_feedback_pipeline.models import Review`, the **parent directory** (`/Users/drewmcgee/workplace/`) needs to be on the module search path — not the project directory itself. That's why you run with `PYTHONPATH=..` at the CLI; it adds the parent to the search path before Python starts.

Pylance (the VS Code language server) does the same static analysis but doesn't know about your `PYTHONPATH=..` runtime trick — it only looks at the workspace root and the venv. So it tries to resolve `yelp_feedback_pipeline.models` starting from `/Users/drewmcgee/workplace/yelp_feedback_pipeline/` and fails, because the package isn't *inside* itself.

`pyrightconfig.json` is Pylance's config file. `extraPaths: [".."]` says "also look one directory up when resolving imports" — exactly replicating what `PYTHONPATH=..` does at runtime. Now both the CLI and the language server agree on where to find the package.

The deeper reason this is needed at all: the project isn't installed as a package (no `pip install -e .`). If it were, Python would register it in the venv's `site-packages` and neither the `PYTHONPATH` hack nor the `extraPaths` config would be necessary. That's the cleaner long-term fix — add a `pyproject.toml` and `pip install -e .` — but `pyrightconfig.json` is the right lightweight fix for now.
so we shouldn't package it yet? does that add extra steps to running or something

Packaging it with `pip install -e .` actually makes running **simpler**, not harder. You'd drop the `PYTHONPATH=..` prefix entirely:

```bash
# now
PYTHONPATH=.. .venv/bin/python -m yelp_feedback_pipeline.pipeline ...

# after packaging
.venv/bin/python -m yelp_feedback_pipeline.pipeline ...
```

And Pylance would resolve imports correctly without `pyrightconfig.json`.

The only thing it adds is a `pyproject.toml` — which is just config, not extra runtime steps. For a project at this stage I'd do it. Want me to add it?

# Yelp Feedback Clustering Pipeline

## Project Overview
Production-oriented NLP pipeline for clustering and labelling Yelp reviews. Uses SentenceTransformer embeddings + BERTopic topic modelling + optional local LLM labelling via Ollama. Designed to be extensible (database, caching, async layers planned later).

## Stack
- **Embeddings**: `sentence-transformers` — `all-MiniLM-L6-v2`, L2-normalised float32, `(n_docs, 384)`
- **Topic modelling**: `BERTopic` with explicit UMAP + HDBSCAN construction
- **LLM labelling**: Ollama only (no Anthropic — keep costs zero). Model: `llama3.2:3b`
- **Config**: Pydantic `BaseSettings`, env prefix `YELP_`, `.env` file supported
- **Serialisation**: safetensors for BERTopic model persistence

## Environment
- Always use `.venv` — system Python is blocked by PEP 668 (Homebrew managed)
- Run with: `PYTHONPATH=.. .venv/bin/python -m yelp_feedback_pipeline.pipeline`
- Ollama runs as a brew service (`brew services start ollama`), model `llama3.2:3b` is pulled

## Project Structure
```
yelp_feedback_pipeline/
  config.py           # PipelineConfig (BaseSettings)
  models.py           # Review, TopicLabel, PipelineResult dataclasses
  pipeline.py         # FeedbackClusteringPipeline + CLI main()
  ingestion/
    loader.py         # Streams NDJSON line-by-line, returns typed DataFrame
  embedding/
    embedder.py       # ReviewEmbedder — lazy-loaded SentenceTransformer
  clustering/
    topic_model.py    # TopicModeller — UMAP + HDBSCAN + BERTopic
  labelling/
    labeller.py       # build_topic_labels(), custom_labeller hook
    llm_labeller.py   # OllamaLabeller, AnthropicLabeller, make_llm_labeller()
  data/               # Put yelp_academic_dataset_review.json here
  output/             # Pipeline outputs: CSVs, embeddings.npy, bertopic_model/
```

## Key Design Decisions
- Pre-computed embeddings passed into BERTopic separately so embedder and topic model are independently cacheable
- `custom_labeller: (keywords, scores) -> str` hook injected into `build_topic_labels()` — LLM is a drop-in
- `LLMLabeller.__call__()` catches all exceptions and falls back to keyword joining — pipeline never fails due to LLM errors
- topic_id == -1 is BERTopic outlier cluster, mapped to label "outlier"
- `nr_topics="auto"` used for automatic topic reduction

## Running the Pipeline
```bash
cd /Users/drewmcgee/workplace/yelp_feedback_pipeline

# Without LLM (keyword labels only)
PYTHONPATH=.. .venv/bin/python -m yelp_feedback_pipeline.pipeline \
  data/yelp_academic_dataset_review.json \
  --max-reviews 50000 \
  --min-topic-size 50 \
  --output-dir output/

# With Ollama LLM labelling (preferred)
PYTHONPATH=.. .venv/bin/python -m yelp_feedback_pipeline.pipeline \
  data/yelp_academic_dataset_review.json \
  --max-reviews 50000 \
  --min-topic-size 50 \
  --llm-labeller ollama \
  --llm-labeller-model llama3.2:3b \
  --output-dir output/
```

## Real Data
- Download from yelp.com/dataset (requires ToS acceptance, ~4GB tar)
- Extract `yelp_academic_dataset_review.json` → place in `data/`
- ~7 million reviews total; start with `--max-reviews 50000` for good topic quality

## Output Files
- `output/labelled_reviews.csv` — reviews with topic_id and topic_label columns
- `output/topic_summary.csv` — one row per topic with label and keyword list
- `output/embeddings.npy` — float32 embedding matrix
- `output/bertopic_model/` — serialised BERTopic model (safetensors)

## Cost Policy
No external API calls. Ollama local only. Do not add Anthropic or any other paid API to the default execution path.

## Git Workflow
After completing any code change (feature, fix, refactor), always commit the work:
1. Stage only the relevant source files (never `.venv/`, `data/`, `output/`, `.env`)
2. Write a concise commit message focused on *why*, not just *what*
3. Commit immediately — do not leave working changes uncommitted

The loop is: code → test → commit. Close it every time.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Yelp Feedback Clustering Pipeline

## Project Overview
Production-oriented NLP pipeline for clustering and labelling Yelp reviews. Uses SentenceTransformer embeddings + BERTopic topic modelling + optional local LLM labelling via Ollama. Designed to be extensible (database, caching, async layers planned later).

## Stack
- **Embeddings**: `sentence-transformers` — `all-MiniLM-L6-v2`, L2-normalised float32, `(n_docs, 384)`
- **Topic modelling**: `BERTopic` with explicit UMAP + HDBSCAN construction
- **LLM labelling**: Ollama only (no Anthropic — keep costs zero). Model: `llama3.2:3b`
- **Config**: Pydantic `BaseSettings`, env prefix `YELP_`, `.env` file supported
- **Serialisation**: safetensors for BERTopic model persistence

## Environment
- Always use `.venv` — system Python is blocked by PEP 668 (Homebrew managed)
- Package is installed in editable mode (`pip install -e .`) — no `PYTHONPATH` prefix needed
- Run with: `.venv/bin/python -m yelp_feedback_pipeline.pipeline`
- Ollama runs as a brew service (`brew services start ollama`), model `llama3.2:3b` is pulled

## Project Structure
```
yelp_feedback_pipeline/
  config.py           # PipelineConfig (BaseSettings)
  models.py           # Review, TopicLabel, PipelineResult dataclasses
  pipeline.py         # FeedbackClusteringPipeline + CLI main()
  ingestion/
    loader.py         # Streams NDJSON line-by-line, returns typed DataFrame
  embedding/
    embedder.py       # ReviewEmbedder — lazy-loaded SentenceTransformer
  clustering/
    topic_model.py    # TopicModeller — UMAP + HDBSCAN + BERTopic
  labelling/
    labeller.py       # build_topic_labels(), custom_labeller hook
    llm_labeller.py   # OllamaLabeller, AnthropicLabeller, make_llm_labeller()
  data/               # Put yelp_academic_dataset_review.json here
  output/             # Pipeline outputs: CSVs, embeddings.npy, bertopic_model/
```

## Key Design Decisions
- Pre-computed embeddings passed into BERTopic separately so embedder and topic model are independently cacheable
- `custom_labeller: (keywords, scores) -> str` hook injected into `build_topic_labels()` — LLM is a drop-in
- `LLMLabeller.__call__()` catches all exceptions and falls back to keyword joining — pipeline never fails due to LLM errors
- topic_id == -1 is BERTopic outlier cluster, mapped to label "outlier"
- `nr_topics="auto"` used for automatic topic reduction

## Running the Pipeline
```bash
cd /Users/drewmcgee/workplace/yelp_feedback_pipeline

# Without LLM (keyword labels only)
PYTHONPATH=.. .venv/bin/python -m yelp_feedback_pipeline.pipeline \
  data/yelp_academic_dataset_review.json \
  --max-reviews 50000 \
  --min-topic-size 50 \
  --output-dir output/

# With Ollama LLM labelling (preferred)
PYTHONPATH=.. .venv/bin/python -m yelp_feedback_pipeline.pipeline \
  data/yelp_academic_dataset_review.json \
  --max-reviews 50000 \
  --min-topic-size 50 \
  --llm-labeller ollama \
  --llm-labeller-model llama3.2:3b \
  --output-dir output/
```

## Real Data
- Download from yelp.com/dataset (requires ToS acceptance, ~4GB tar)
- Extract `yelp_academic_dataset_review.json` → place in `data/`
- ~7 million reviews total; start with `--max-reviews 50000` for good topic quality

## Output Files
- `output/labelled_reviews.csv` — reviews with topic_id and topic_label columns
- `output/topic_summary.csv` — one row per topic with label and keyword list
- `output/embeddings.npy` — float32 embedding matrix
- `output/bertopic_model/` — serialised BERTopic model (safetensors)

## Cost Policy
No external API calls. Ollama local only. Do not add Anthropic or any other paid API to the default execution path.

## Git Workflow
After completing any code change (feature, fix, refactor), always commit the work:
1. Stage only the relevant source files (never `.venv/`, `data/`, `output/`, `.env`)
2. Write a concise commit message focused on *why*, not just *what*
3. Commit immediately — do not leave working changes uncommitted

The loop is: code → test → commit. Close it every time.
